front: What is the formal definition of a limit point (or accumulation point) in a metric space (X, d)?
back: A point x ∈ X is a limit point of a subset A ⊆ X if every open ball centered at x contains at least one point of A different from x itself. Formally: For every ε > 0, the intersection B(x, ε) ∩ (A \ {x}) ≠ ∅.

front: What is the definition of an isolated point in a metric space?
back: A point a ∈ A is an isolated point of A if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(a, ε) contains no other points of A. In other words, B(a, ε) ∩ A = {a}.

front: How are the set of limit points of A (denoted A') and the closure of A (denoted Ā or cl(A)) related?
back: The closure of a set A is equal to the union of A and its set of limit points. Formally: Ā = A ∪ A'.

front: True or False: A set is closed if and only if it contains all of its limit points.
back: True. This is a fundamental theorem in metric spaces. A set F is closed if and only if F' ⊆ F.

front: What is the key characteristic that distinguishes a limit point from an isolated point within a set?
back: A limit point can be approximated arbitrarily closely by other points in the set, while an isolated point has a neighborhood around it that is devoid of any other points from the set.
